reflection.quote: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
reflection.reading:
It's a Thursday in late July. Nothing dramatic on the calendar. The weekend hasn't started, the week isn't done. This is the kind of day I used to white-knuckle through in early sobriety, waiting for something to happen. Turns out these are the days that actually build a life.

When I was drinking, I lived for the peaks — Friday night, the trip, the party, whatever was going to lift me out of the flat middle of my own week. Everything else was just waiting. And because I was always waiting, I was never actually there. Sobriety flipped that. The Thursday coffee, the drive home from work with the windows down, the boring gym session, the text back to a friend I might have ghosted five years ago — that's where the life is. Not in the highlight reel. In the reps.

Durant was paraphrasing Aristotle, and Aristotle was pointing at something I keep having to relearn: I don't become someone through one big decision. I become someone through what I do on the Thursdays. Show up sober today, answer the email, call my mom back, go to bed at a reasonable hour, and something quietly compounds. Ten years in, I can tell you the compound interest is real. But it only pays out if you keep making the deposits.
reflection.meditation: What is one small, ordinary thing you can do well today — not for anyone else, just because it's yours to do?

affirmations: I am building a life in the small hours, not just the big moments.
Today I choose presence over performance.
I have the capacity to respond instead of react.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a Thursday I get to remember. Grateful for the boring parts — the routine, the quiet, the fact that nothing is on fire. There was a version of me who couldn't have imagined finding this much in a plain summer day, and I owe him this one.
